# Reconcile superseded main deployments

A newer waiting Release Plan may supersede an older waiting plan, but a running environment deployment is never cancelled. When the newer plan acquires that environment's lock, it rereads Environment State and compares the complete desired owning-workspace tag map at its successfully released commit boundary with the last successful tag recorded independently for every Deployment Unit. This lets one plan deliver changes accumulated across several merges without losing a unit from a displaced or partially failed run.

A unit advances its Environment State only after its deployment succeeds; failure to persist the state fails the job, while missing, malformed, or stale state selects the unit again. The planner expands delivery dependants from the Deployment Catalog after comparison, so consumers are reconciled with changed dependencies. Develop and production maintain independent state. The mechanism coalesces queued work but does not permit an older running deployment to overwrite or cancel a newer one: environment-level serialization ensures the newer plan rereads any tag written by the older run before planning.

Environment State uses GitHub Deployments rather than a new AWS persistence service. Each unit deployment records its exact owning-workspace Release tag as the deployment ref, the long-lived target as the environment, and the Deployment Unit as the task; only the latest successful record for that environment and task is considered current. The workflow uses `deployments: write`, disables deployment auto-merge and required-context evaluation because the exact released ref has already passed the pull-request gates, and links statuses to the originating Actions run. Failed attempts remain auditable without advancing state, and expected CloudFormation stacks are still queried where applicable so a successful historical record cannot hide a missing runtime stack.
